summary: This post walks through using gdb to inspect the stack of a simple C program, showing exactly how variables are laid out in memory. Evans demonstrates how to find variable addresses, examine raw bytes, and understand the difference between stack-allocated and heap-allocated strings. The walkthrough deliberately uses the unsafe `gets` function to demonstrate a buffer overflow, showing how writing past a buffer's bounds overwrites adjacent memory. The post also covers how stack smashing protection detects overflows using canary values, and how /proc/PID/maps reveals where the stack and heap live in memory. Evans argues that hands-on exploration with gdb makes abstract concepts like the stack, heap, and pointers much more concrete.
key_points: You can use gdb's `x` command to examine raw memory and see exactly how variables like strings, integers, and pointers are laid out on the stack
Stack-allocated strings store their contents directly on the stack, while heap-allocated strings store a pointer on the stack that references data elsewhere in memory
Buffer overflows happen when you write past a buffer's declared size, overwriting adjacent memory—demonstrated here by writing 13 bytes into a 10-byte array
The compiler's stack smashing protector uses canary values to detect when a buffer overflow has corrupted the stack
The stack and heap occupy different address ranges visible in /proc/PID/maps, making it easy to distinguish stack addresses (0x7fffff...) from heap addresses (0x5555...)
money_quotes: We learn more when things go wrong.

I really recommend playing around with gdb like this – even if you don't understand every single thing that you see in memory, I find that actually seeing the data in my program's memory like this makes these abstract concepts like 'the stack' and 'the heap' and 'pointers' a lot easier to understand.
This is a classic buffer overflow, and what's happening is that stack_string wrote over other data from the program.
It can crash your program or, worse, open you up to Very Bad Security Problems.
The weird thing about this is that stack_string was only supposed to be 10 bytes. But now suddenly we've put 13 bytes in it?
These bytes actually get read backwards because x86 is little-endian.
Playing about with gdb like this is really helpful.
key_insight: Actually examining raw memory with gdb transforms abstract concepts like the stack, heap, and buffer overflows into something concrete and understandable.
